data.history: The Al Hamoud Al Nuaimi family represents a distinguished branch of the Nuaimi tribal confederation with documented historical significance in the Al Buraimi region of northern Oman and the UAE border area. According to historical records from the Al-Buraimi area, the family played a prominent role in the governance and rebuilding of the region during the late 18th and 19th centuries. The rebuilding of Al Buraimi saw rulers such as Sultan bin Mohammed Al Hamoud Al Qurtasi Al Nuaimi and later, Sheikh Saqr bin Sultan bin Mohammed Al Hamoud Al Qurtasi Al Nuaimi serving as the regional leaders. The Al Hamoud branch was particularly influential during the period when Mohammed bin Ali and his allies worked to establish peace agreements in territorial conflicts with neighboring powers. Historical documentation shows that Mohammed bin Ali had strong ties with the Bani Jaber tribe in Sa'ara, eventually assuming leadership around 1290 AH (approximately 1873-1874 CE). The Al Hamoud family's rule persisted until 1950, with Sheikh Saqr bin Sultan bin Mohammed Al Hamoud serving as the final ruler of this line. The family's influence extended across the broader Buraimi oasis region, which historically consisted of nine villages including Sa'ara, Hamasah, Haili, Al Jaimi, Al Mawijai, Al Mu'tarid, Al Jahili, Al Ain, and Al Qutayrah, and served as an important commercial and cultural center. Their legacy is preserved in the historical fortresses and administrative structures of Al-Buraimi, most notably Al-Hillah Fort which was built by Sheikh Mohammed bin Ali bin Hamoud Al-Nuaimi and served as a center for local administration, replacing the earlier Al Khandaq Fort to meet the needs of regional governance.
data.description: The Al Hamoud Al Nuaimi family is a distinguished branch of the Nuaimi tribal confederation that historically ruled the Al Buraimi region until 1950. They are known for their administrative and political leadership in the northern emirates and Oman border region, with their legacy preserved in historical fortresses and governance structures.
data.originStory: The Al Hamoud branch of the Nuaimi tribe established themselves as rulers in the Al Buraimi oasis region, with documented leadership from the late 18th century through 1950. Their origins trace to the broader Nuaimi tribal confederation, with the Al Hamoud lineage becoming prominent through their role in regional governance and peace-making during territorial conflicts.

data.history: The Al Nuaimi family is the ruling dynasty of the Emirate of Ajman in the UAE, descended from the Al Bu Kharaiban section of the Na'im tribal confederation. The family's rise to power began when Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi and fifty of his followers took the coastal settlement of Ajman from members of the Al Bu Shamis tribe in a short conflict. Rule was formally established when the Ajman fort fell to Rashid's followers in 1816 or 1817, with his authority endorsed by the powerful Sheikh Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi of neighboring Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah. The Na'im tribe's traditional heartland was the Buraimi oasis and nearby Al Ain, where they had expanded at the expense of the Dhawahir tribe and at their peak in 1818 numbered around 20,000 men according to British records. The ruling line has maintained continuous governance since 1816, with succession passing through various members of the family including multiple rulers named Rashid and Humaid. Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi III ruled from 1928 to 1981, overseeing Ajman's transition from a small fishing and pearling settlement through the discovery of oil and into the modern UAE federation established in 1971. The current ruler, Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III, succeeded his father in 1981 and has presided over Ajman's transformation into a modern commercial center. The family has maintained close relationships with other UAE ruling families, including intermarriage connections - notably, the current ruler's sister was the late Sheikha Fatima bint Rashid Al Nuaimi, whose son is the ruler of Fujairah, Sheikh Hamad Al Sharqi. During the period leading up to UAE independence, Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi III was among the founding fathers who supported the union, though he initially expressed some dissatisfaction with the representation allocated to the smaller emirates.
data.description: The Al Nuaimi family is the ruling dynasty of the Emirate of Ajman in the UAE, having governed since 1816. data.history: The Al ash-Sheikh family are the descendants of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab al-Tamimi al-Najdi (1703-1792), founder of the Wahhabi movement and member of the Banu Tamim tribe. Born in Nejd in 1703, Ibn Abd al-Wahhab moved to Diriyah in the 1740s where he formed a historic alliance with Muhammad ibn Saud in 1744, creating the religious-political foundation of what would become Saudi Arabia. This pact established a power-sharing arrangement where the Al Saud would maintain political authority while the Al ash-Sheikh would lead religious affairs and legitimize royal rule through their moral authority. The family suffered major setbacks when Ottoman forces executed many members in 1818, including Sulayman ibn Abd Allah, a grandson of Ibn Abd al-Wahhab, and exiled others to Egypt. However, they survived and continued their religious leadership role through the Second Saudi State and into the modern kingdom established in 1932. The family has maintained extensive intermarriage with the Al Saud, including King Faisal's mother Tarfa bint Abdullah Al Sheikh, cementing their position as Saudi Arabia's premier religious family, second in prestige only to the royal family. While their dominance has somewhat diminished in recent decades due to an influx of scholars from other families, they continue to hold key religious positions including the Grand Mufti and various ministerial roles. Notable modern members include Turki Al-Sheikh, chairman of the General Entertainment Authority and close adviser to Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, and the late Grand Mufti Abdulaziz ibn Abdullah Al ash-Sheikh who served until his death in 2025.
data.description: The Al ash-Sheikh family are the descendants of Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, founder of the Wahhabi movement, and serve as Saudi Arabia's premier religious authority. They have maintained their role as the kingdom's chief Islamic scholars and jurists for over 275 years through their historic alliance with the Al Saud family.
data.originStory: The family originates from Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, who established the Wahhabi reform movement in 18th century Najd. Their alliance with the Al Saud family in 1744 created the religious-political foundation of the Saudi state.
data.modernStatus: Continue to hold key religious and government positions including Minister of Islamic Affairs (Saleh bin Abdul-Aziz Al ash-Sheikh), Minister of Municipal and Rural Affairs (Abdullatif bin Abdulmalik bin Omar Al-ash Sheikh), and Minister of Entertainment (Turki Al-Sheikh). Members serve as judges, religious scholars, and government officials throughout Saudi Arabia.
data.nameEtymology: The Arabic name Al ash-Sheikh (آل الشيخ) translates into English as family of the Sheikh or House of the Sheikh. The word Al, in conjunction with the name of an ancestor, means family of or House of. The term ash-Sheikh refers to the Islamic religious reformer Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, the family's ancestor. He was known as the Sheikh, a term of respect for a noted elder, teacher or religious leader.

data.history: The Al-Qarooni/Karuni family represents one of the Baharna subgroups with a documented history of recurring migrations between Bahrain and Khuzestan province in Iran. The family is part of the broader Baharna ethnoreligious group of Shia Muslim Arabs indigenous to the historical region of Bahrain, who are considered by some scholars as among the original inhabitants of Eastern Arabia predating the 18th-century arrival of the Banu Utbah. According to the research, the Al-Qarooni/Karuni family had to re-migrate after the Persian harassment campaign against the Arabs of Mohammerah (Khorramshahr) intensified, particularly following the 1951 events when Persian authorities targeted ethnic Arabs in the region. This migration pattern reflects the broader Baharna experience of displacement due to political persecution and sectarian tensions that began in earnest during the 17th-18th centuries when various Baharna families sought refuge in Khuzestan during periods of instability in Bahrain under successive rulers. The family maintained their cultural and linguistic identity throughout these migrations, speaking a variant of Mesopotamian Arabic that distinguishes them within the broader Baharna community. Some genetic samples suggest an affinity to the J2 Haplogroup, specifically the J-BY44557 branch, which aligns with the broader Baharna genetic profile showing closer affinities to populations of Iraq, the Levant, Iran and the Caucasus rather than central Arabian Peninsula tribes. Their story exemplifies the complex transnational identity of Gulf Arab families who maintained connections across political boundaries while adapting to changing circumstances in both Bahrain and Iranian Khuzestan.
data.description: The Al-Qarooni/Karuni family is a Baharna Shia Arab family with documented migrations between Bahrain and Iranian Khuzestan, speaking a variant of Mesopotamian Arabic and maintaining cultural ties across the Gulf region despite political displacement.
data.originStory: Part of the broader Baharna ethnoreligious group indigenous to the historical region of Bahrain, with recurring migrations between Bahrain and Khuzestan due to political persecution and sectarian tensions across multiple centuries.

data.history: The Nonoo family traces its origins to Basra, Iraq, having moved to Bahrain over a century ago along with hundreds of other Iraqi Jews seeking economic opportunities in the thriving Gulf trading centers. According to one account, Houda Nonoo's grandfather Ibrahim left Baghdad in 1888 and started a financial business in Bahrain, though the family's broader origins point to Basra specifically. The family became part of a significant Jewish merchant community that had deep historical roots in the Arabian Gulf region, with Jewish presence in the area documented back to ancient times and Arabic sources recording Jews in Bahrain's old capital of Hajar at the time of Islamic conquest in 630 CE. The modern Bahraini Jewish community, including the Nonoos, faced severe challenges during the mid-20th century riots in 1947-48 following violence in Palestine, when a mob attacked Jewish homes and shops in Manama, destroyed the synagogue, and murdered an elderly Jewish woman, forcing many families to flee to Bombay, Israel, the UK, or United States. However, the Nonoos remained and became increasingly prominent in Bahraini society. By 1934, Abraham Nonoo became the first Jewish member of the Manama Municipality, marking early political participation. The family established Basma Company, which has been passed down through generations and continues to operate today offering various office services. The family achieved remarkable political prominence in modern Bahrain when Ebrahim Daoud Nonoo (born 1960) served as the first Jewish member of the Shura Council from 2001-2006 and currently heads both the House of the Ten Commandments Synagogue and the Jewish community of Bahrain as president of the Association of Gulf Jewish Communities. His cousin Houda Nonoo made international history as Bahrain's Ambassador to the United States from 2008-2013, becoming the first Jewish ambassador of any Middle Eastern Arab country. The family has also achieved international recognition through fashion designer Misha Nonoo, who was born in Bahrain to a Jewish father of Iraqi descent and English mother, later establishing herself in New York and London fashion circles with connections to British royalty.
data.description: The Nonoo family is a prominent Iraqi Jewish merchant family that settled in Bahrain over a century ago, achieving remarkable success in business, politics, and diplomacy while maintaining leadership of Bahrain's small Jewish community.
data.originStory: Iraqi Jewish merchants originally from Basra who migrated to Bahrain over a century ago as part of a larger movement of Iraqi Jews seeking economic opportunities in the Gulf's thriving trading centers. One family account states that Ibrahim Nonoo left Baghdad in 1888 to start a financial business in Bahrain.
data.modernStatus: The family operates Basma Company with Ebrahim Daoud Nonoo as CEO, while he also serves as head of Bahrain's Jewish community and president of the Association of Gulf Jewish Communities. Fashion designer Misha Nonoo runs a successful international fashion label and is married to oil heir Michael Hess.

data.history: The Al Rostamani family began their business journey in 1951 when Abdul Wahid and his brother Abdullah Hassan, two sons of a local pearl trader, established a family business on the shore of Dubai Creek. In 1954, they formally founded the AW Rostamani Group and opened Dubai's first bookshop, Al Ahliya Library, importing books from Egypt, Syria, and Lebanon through Gulf Air via Bahrain. The brothers successfully transitioned from their father's traditional pearl trading background to modern commercial enterprises. In 1957, they established a General Trading company, and by 1968 had founded Arabian Automobiles as exclusive distributors of Nissan, INFINITI, and Renault in Dubai and the Northern Emirates. The family's business empire expanded significantly over the decades, diversifying into real estate through AWR Properties (which has managed property development since 1982), logistics, retail, lighting, travel, and agritech sectors. Notable developments include the 21st Century Tower on Sheikh Zayed Road and the Al Rostamani Maze Tower completed in 2011. Abdullah Al Rostamani became a prominent figure in UAE media, purchasing Gulf News in 1984 alongside Obaid Humaid Al Tayer and Juma Al Majid, forming Al Nisr Publishing. After Abdullah's death in 2006, the family business continued under new leadership. Today, the AW Rostamani Group employs over 4,000 workers and operates across the GCC region, with Khalid Abdul Wahid Al Rostamani serving as Chairman and Dr. Amina Al Rustamani as Director, Board Member, and CEO of AWR Properties.

Founded in 1954, the AW Rostamani Group employs over 4,000 workers and operates across the GCC region.

Led by Chairman Khalid Abdul Wahid Al Rostamani and Dr. Amina Al Rustamani as AWR Properties CEO.

data.history: The Al Khoory family is a prominent merchant family of Iranian/Persian origin who settled in the Dubai Creek area during the early 19th century, as part of the broader wave of Persian traders who established themselves across Gulf port cities. The family name derives from 'khouri,' the Arabic word meaning 'priest,' which comes from the Latin word 'curia' or possibly from the French 'curé.' However, in the UAE context, the Al Khoory family were Iranian merchants rather than clergy, and their name evolved from their association with the Dubai Creek settlement area (Al Hor/Al Hour). The Iranian merchant community, including the Al Khoory family, played a foundational role in Dubai's commercial development, with Persian traders being among the first major groups of foreign settlers in the region, documented from the 1810s onwards. The Al Khoory family established trading operations along the creek that connected the Arabian interior with maritime trade routes to Persia, India, and East Africa. Through the 20th century, the family diversified from traditional trading into modern business sectors, becoming particularly prominent in luxury retail by securing the Rolex concession for the UAE. They also expanded into hospitality with Al Khoory Hotels, real estate development, and general trading. The family maintains a significant presence in Dubai's business community today, with operations spanning multiple sectors. Their trajectory mirrors that of many Persian-origin merchant families in the Gulf who leveraged early commercial networks into diversified modern business empires, representing the multicultural commercial heritage of Dubai where Persian, Indian, and Arab merchant families together built the trading hub that became one of the world's most dynamic cities.

The family name evolved from their association with the creek settlement area.

data.history: The Al Turki name in the Gulf region represents multiple distinct lineages rather than a single unified merchant family. The most prominent Al Turki figures documented are primarily associated with the Saudi royal family and government positions. Prince Turki bin Faisal Al Saud (born 1945) served as head of Saudi Arabia's General Intelligence Presidency from 1979 to 2001 and later as ambassador to the UK and United States. He is the son of King Faisal and grandson of Saudi Arabia's founder King Abdulaziz. Another significant figure is Sultana bint Turki Al Sudairi (c. 1940-2011), who was the first wife of Prince Salman bin Abdulaziz (later King Salman) and daughter of Turki Al Sudairi, former governor of Asir Province. Her father served as governor from the early days of the Kingdom until 1969. The Al Sudairi branch represents a powerful clan within Saudi Arabia that frequently intermarried with the Al Saud royal family. A separate case involves Homaidan Ali Al-Turki (born 1969), a Saudi national who was convicted in Colorado in 2006 on charges of sexual assault and human trafficking of his Indonesian housekeeper, receiving a 28-year sentence later reduced to 8 years. This case strained US-Saudi relations and sparked controversy in Saudi Arabia, where many viewed him as a victim of anti-Muslim bias. The research reveals no evidence of a unified Al Turki merchant family with documented origins in Najd that migrated to establish trading operations in the coastal regions, as suggested in some sources.
data.description: Al Turki represents multiple distinct lineages in Saudi Arabia and the Gulf, primarily associated with the Saudi royal family and government positions rather than a unified merchant dynasty.
data.originStory: Origins disputed - the Al Turki name appears across multiple distinct Saudi lineages with no clear evidence of a unified family origin. Documented figures are primarily connected to the Saudi royal family or individual cases rather than a cohesive merchant family background.
data.modernStatus: Various Al Turki individuals hold positions within Saudi royal circles and government, but no unified business empire or merchant operations have been documented in the research.

data.history:
The Al Ghanim family is one of Kuwait's most prominent merchant dynasties, tracing their origins to the Bani Utbah confederation that migrated from Najd to Kuwait in the mid-18th century. As part of the founding elite of Kuwait alongside the Al Sabah ruling family, they established themselves as successful pearl merchants and maritime traders in the emerging commercial hub. The family has maintained close genealogical and political connections to Kuwait's ruling Al Sabah family through strategic marriages - notably, Emir Nawaf Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah married Sharifa Sulaiman Al-Jasem Al-Ghanim, daughter of Sulaiman Al-Jasem Al-Ghanim. Additionally, Ahmed, son of former Prime Minister Jaber Al-Mubarak Al-Hamad Al-Sabah, married the daughter of Ibrahim bin Muhammed Al Ghanim, further cementing these elite connections.

The family successfully transitioned from traditional maritime commerce and pearl trading to modern diversified business enterprises. The Ali Alghanim & Sons Company, led by Ali Mohammed Thunayan Al-Ghanim (father of parliamentary speaker Marzouq Al-Ghanim), became a multi-line conglomerate that includes BMW and Land Rover dealerships in Kuwait. Ali Al-Ghanim also served as former chairman of the Kuwait Chamber of Commerce and Industry. His brother, Fouad Mohammed Thunayan Alghanim, chairs the Fouad Alghanim & Sons Group of Companies, a multi-line conglomerate with subsidiaries across the U.S., Europe, Africa and the Middle East.

The family's political influence is exemplified by Marzouq Ali Mohammed Al-Ghanim (born 1968), who served as Speaker of the Kuwaiti National Assembly from 2013 and held numerous regional parliamentary leadership positions including President of the GCC Parliamentary Union (2018-2023) and President of the Arab Inter-Parliamentary Union (2013-2016). The son of Ali al-Ghanim and Faiza al-Kharafi (from the prominent Kharafi merchant family), Marzouq earned a mechanical engineering degree from Seattle University and worked for Boubyan Petrochemical Company before entering politics in 2006. His political career has been marked by strong anti-Israeli stances, including his 2017 confrontation with Israeli delegates at an Inter-Parliamentary Union meeting in Russia and his symbolic rejection of the U.S. 'Deal of the Century' in 2020.

Historically, the Al Ghanim name has deep Arabian roots, with research indicating connections to medieval Islamic figures. The 12th-century records mention Ghanim ibn Ali ibn Hussein al-Ansari al-Khazraj, who was entrusted by Sultan Saladin with custody of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in Jerusalem around 1192, though this appears to be a different lineage from the Kuwaiti Al Ghanim family. The family has also established presences in other Gulf states, with the Old Al Ghanim district in Doha, Qatar, named after the Al Ghanim branch of the Al Bin Ali tribe, indicating broader Gulf connections and migration patterns among merchant families in the region.
data.description: The Al Ghanim family is one of Kuwait's leading merchant dynasties and political families, descended from the Bani Utbah confederation that helped found Kuwait in the 18th century. They operate major business conglomerates including automotive dealerships and maintain close ties to the ruling Al Sabah family through strategic marriages and political positions.
data.originStory: The Al Ghanim family migrated from Najd to Kuwait in the mid-18th century as part of the Bani Utbah confederation movement that founded Kuwait. They were among the founding elite alongside the Al Sabah ruling family and established themselves as successful pearl merchants and maritime traders.
data.modernStatus: Major business conglomerates including Ali Alghanim & Sons Company (BMW/Land Rover dealerships) and Fouad Alghanim & Sons Group with international subsidiaries. Significant political influence through parliamentary leadership positions and close ties to ruling family.

data.history: The Al Kazim family traces its lineage to Musa al-Kazim (745-799 CE), the seventh Imam in Twelver Shia Islam and a direct descendant of the Islamic prophet Muhammad through Ali ibn Abi Talib and Fatima. Musa al-Kazim was born in Medina to Ja'far al-Sadiq, the sixth Shia Imam, and spent most of his life under severe restrictions by the Abbasid caliphs. He established an underground network of representatives throughout the Abbasid Empire to organize Shia affairs and collect religious donations. Al-Kazim died in 799 CE in a Baghdad prison, likely poisoned by order of Harun al-Rashid, and was buried in what is now Kazimayn, Baghdad. His son Ali al-Rida succeeded him as the eighth Imam and was notably appointed heir apparent by the Abbasid caliph al-Mamun in 817 CE before dying mysteriously in 818 CE in Tus (present-day Mashhad, Iran). The Al Kazim lineage has produced numerous Islamic scholars across Iraq, Iran, and Lebanon, with the Al-Sadr family being a prominent branch that traces its descent through 27 generations back to Musa al-Kazim. The Al-Sadr family emerged from Jabal Amel in Lebanon as a branch of the Charafeddine family, itself part of the Noureddine family lineage. This scholarly family has produced notable figures like Mohammad Baqir al-Sadr (1935-1980), executed by Saddam Hussein's regime, and Muqtada al-Sadr, who leads a major militia in Iraq. The family has maintained significant religious and political influence across the Shia world, with members serving as prominent clerics, political leaders, and martyrs in the struggle for Shia rights.

data.history: The Al Juffali family originates from Unayzah in Al-Qassim province of Najd, and are members of the Bani Khalid dynasty, who ruled Eastern Arabia from the early 1600s to the late 1800s before suffering military defeats against the Saudis. The family claims descent from Khālid ibn al-Walīd, the famous Arab military commander, though like other Bani Khalid lineage claims, this has been questioned by Arab genealogists who suggest they may descend from his relatives from Banu Makhzum rather than Khalid himself directly. The modern business empire was founded by Abdullah bin Ibrahim Al Juffali, whose sons initially managed the family's electricity operations that later became the Saudi Electricity Company. The most transformative figure was Ahmed bin Abdullah Al Juffali (1924-1994), who established E.A. Juffali and Brothers in 1946, building it into Saudi Arabia's largest privately owned enterprise. Ahmed was instrumental in founding SAMA (Saudi Arabian Monetary Authority) and served on boards of international companies including IBM and Wells Fargo. The company expanded rapidly during Saudi Arabia's oil boom, introducing Mercedes-Benz, Siemens, IBM, and numerous other multinational brands to the Saudi market. After Ahmed's death in 1994, leadership passed to his sons Walid (1955-2016) and Khaled (born 1958). Walid became chairman of Saudi American Bank and developed significant interests in neuroscience, earning a PhD from Imperial College in 2012, while also hosting MBC's reality show 'The Investor' in 2006. His high-profile divorce from Christina Estrada in 2016 resulted in a £75 million settlement, the largest in English legal history at the time. Khaled serves as managing partner and vice chairman, continuing to expand the business empire across multiple industries including automotive, chemicals, and manufacturing.
data.description: The Al Juffali family from Unayzah, Al-Qassim, built Saudi Arabia's largest private enterprise, E.A. Juffali and Brothers, founded in 1946. The company represents major international brands including Mercedes-Benz, Siemens, and IBM, spanning multiple industries from automotive to chemicals and manufacturing.

data.modernStatus: Controls E.A. Juffali and Brothers, Saudi Arabia's largest privately owned enterprise with 49,218 employees worldwide, representing major international brands. data.history: The Al Issa family represents multiple distinct lineages across the Middle East and Arab world, with the most prominent being the Palestinian Christian El-Issa family from Jaffa. The El-Issa family emerged in 20th century Palestine as distinguished journalists and intellectuals. The family was founded by Issa El-Issa and his cousin Yousef El-Issa, who established the influential newspaper Falastin in Jaffa in 1909. This publication became one of the first to warn about the Zionist movement in Mandatory Palestine, positioning the family as early voices of Palestinian nationalism. The family's journalistic legacy expanded through numerous publications including Al-Asma'i (1908-1909), Alif Bā' (1930-1958), and Al-Bilād. Following the displacement of Palestinians, the family continued their media work in Jordan and Syria, with Issa's son Raja El-Issa managing the newspaper after his father's death and becoming the first chairman of the Jordan Press Association in Amman in 1956. Separately, the Al Issa name appears in the UAE royal context through Issa bin Zayed Al Nahyan, son of former UAE President Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan and member of the ruling House of Nahyan. Born to Sheikha Amna bint Salah Al Badi, Issa is a half-brother to Presidents Khalifa bin Zayed Al Nahyan and Mohammed bin Zayed Al Nahyan. Despite his royal status, he holds no official government position and operates as a real estate developer, owning Al Hekma Tower in Dubai. The name also appears among Yemeni performers like Issa Al-Laith, a Zaidi vocalist born in 1985 who became affiliated with the Houthi movement.

data.history:
The Al Nafisi family represents multiple distinguished lineages across the Gulf region with distinct origins and remarkable contributions to politics, academia, medicine, and literature. The Kuwaiti branch traces its lineage to the Al-Nafsiah family that originated from the Mutayr tribe, establishing their presence in Freej Soud, Jibla, Kuwait. This branch is exemplified by Abdullah Fahad Al-Nafisi (born 1945), who became a distinguished academic and politician, earning his PhD from Cambridge University in 1972 and serving as a member of Kuwait's National Assembly in 1985. His political career was marked by opposition activities, including interrogating government ministers about oil contracts, which contributed to the dissolution of parliament in 1986. Abdullah Al-Nafisi worked as a professor of political science at Kuwait University and was promoted to president of the College of Political Science in 1974, but was fired in 1978 following the release of his controversial book 'Kuwait, the other opinion,' which led to his passport being confiscated for two years.

Parallel to the Kuwaiti branch exists a distinguished Persian intellectual lineage from Kerman, Iran, whose medical tradition traces back to Burhan-ud-din Kermani, a 15th-century court physician for Ulugh Beg. This Iranian branch is exemplified by Mirza Ali Akbar Khan Nafisi (1847-1924), known as Nazem al-Atebba, who became one of Iran's most prominent doctors during the late Qajar era. Born in Kerman on 12 March 1847, he graduated from Dar ul-Funun medical school in 1872 and served as personal physician to Mozaffar ad-Din Shah, playing a crucial role in Iran's Constitutional Revolution by persuading the Shah to sign the Constitutional Decree. Nazem al-Atebba established several European-style hospitals in Tehran and Mashhad and founded the first assembly of experts for health in Iranian history.

The Iranian Nafisi family's scholarly contributions extended through generations, with Nazem al-Atebba's son Saeed Nafisi becoming a celebrated scholar, and another son Ali-Asghar Nafisi serving as Iran's first Minister of Health. This intellectual legacy continued with Azar Nafisi (born 1948), the daughter of Ahmad Nafisi (former mayor of Tehran from 1961-1963) and niece of Saeed Nafisi, who became a renowned Iranian-American writer and professor best known for her 2003 book 'Reading Lolita in Tehran.' After the Iranian Revolution, Azar Nafisi taught English literature at the University of Tehran until 1981, when she was expelled for refusing to wear the mandatory Islamic veil. She later taught at Allameh Tabataba'I University and conducted private literary discussions with female students at her home from 1995-1997 before emigrating to the United States.

The family's literary and medical achievements are particularly notable, with Nazem al-Atebba producing the comprehensive four-volume Persian lexicon 'Farhang-e Nafisi,' which was considered the most comprehensive Persian dictionary before Dehkhoda's work. He spent over 25 years on this project, even learning English at age 55 to access necessary resources. The family's contributions span from establishing Iran's first modern hospitals and health institutions to producing internationally acclaimed literature that bridges Eastern and Western cultures, demonstrating the complex ethnic and cultural heritage characteristic of distinguished Gulf and Persian intellectual families.
data.description: Al Nafisi represents multiple distinguished lineages across the Gulf region, including a Kuwaiti branch descended from the Mutayr tribe and a Persian branch from Kerman, Iran, both contributing significantly to politics, academia, medicine, and literature throughout the 19th, 20th, and 21st centuries.

data.history:
The Abookara (Al Kuwari) emerge in British colonial records as one of the most significant tribal groups in northern Qatar during the 19th century. According to George Barnes Brucks' 1820s survey, they were 'formerly one of the most powerful tribes in these parts, but now incorporated with the Uttoobees (Bani Utbah).' This incorporation suggests a complex political relationship where the tribe maintained its identity while being allied with or subject to the larger Bani Utbah confederation. The tribe had substantial settlements across multiple locations in northern Qatar, with approximately 450 members in Al Huwaila (then the peninsula's principal town), 150 in Fuwayrit, 100 in Al Ruwais, and 50 in Abu Dhalouf, all described as 'subject to Bahrein.'

The Abookara were primarily engaged in maritime economic activities that formed the backbone of the Gulf economy. They were noted as fishermen involved in pearling and coastal trade, with their settlements strategically positioned along Qatar's northern coastline. J.G. Lorimer's early 20th century Gazetteer confirms their continued presence, recording approximately 100 houses of the Bu Kuwara in Fuwayrit and noting their presence in Al Ruwais before 1856. The tribe maintained significant maritime assets, with Lorimer documenting about 35 pearl boats manned by 420 men, 9 other sea-going vessels, and 12 fishing boats belonging to Fuwayrit alone.

The tribe experienced political tensions with the emerging Al Thani authority in the late 19th century. In November 1879, there was a mass exodus of Bu Kuwara members from Doha to Fuwayrit due to tensions with Emir Jassim bin Mohammed, reportedly instigated by the Bahraini sheikh. This migration demonstrates the tribe's continued allegiance to Bahrain rather than the consolidating Qatari leadership. By 1950, anthropological fieldwork recorded approximately 600 tents belonging to the Bu Kuwara tribe in the Fuwayrit area, indicating their adaptation to changing political circumstances while maintaining their traditional semi-nomadic lifestyle.

Throughout the historical record, the Abookara appear to have maintained their distinct identity while navigating the complex political landscape of the Gulf. Their consistent presence in northern Qatar's coastal settlements, from Al Huwaila in the west to multiple towns along the northeastern coast, suggests they controlled or had significant influence over important pearling and fishing grounds. The tribe's incorporation with the Bani Utbah and subject status to Bahrain reflects the broader political dynamics of the Gulf, where smaller tribal groups often aligned with larger powers while maintaining their local autonomy and traditional economic activities.

data.history: The research provided contains extremely limited verified historical information about Al Abdul Dayem as a tribal grouping. The only concrete reference appears in Wikipedia's article about the Mahamid tribe, which lists 'Al Abdul Dayem' as one of the branches that emerged from the Mahamid in Jordan. According to this source, the Mahamid are described as an Arab tribe tracing origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb, with original residences in Yemen and the Hijaz between Mecca and Medina. The Mahamid allegedly migrated from their original homeland when their numbers grew too large for their territory in Yemen, Najd, and the Hijaz, eventually settling in the Karak region of Jordan after passing through Dumat Al-Jandal in the north. The Mahamid established what is called the 'Emirate of Mahamid' in the Karak region, though some historians described their rule as harsh, leading to opposition from other clans. However, the specific role of Al Abdul Dayem within this tribal structure, their particular genealogical connections, geographical distribution, migrations, or historical significance as a distinct branch remains undocumented in the available research. Without additional verified sources about their lineage, political activities, or modern status within Arabian Peninsula tribal networks, their exact historical trajectory and significance cannot be established from the research provided.

The research provided discusses the Bahri Mamluks (al-Mamalik al-Baḥariyya), a medieval Egyptian dynasty that ruled from 1250-1382 CE, rather than the Arabian tribal group Al-Bahri. The Bahri Mamluks were named 'Bahri' (meaning 'of the river') after their original barracks on Roda Island in the Nile River in Cairo, not due to any maritime activities. This dynasty consisted primarily of Kipchak Turkic mamluks (freed slave soldiers) who established the Mamluk Sultanate after overthrowing the Ayyubid dynasty in Egypt in 1250. The Bahri Mamluks were of mostly Kipchak Turkic origin, with fourteen of eighteen sultans between 1279 and 1390 belonging to the Qalawunid lineage. After 1382/1390, they were succeeded by a second Mamluk regime, the Burji Mamluks, who were largely of Circassian origin. The research contains no information about the Arabian Peninsula tribal group Al-Bahri mentioned in the existing data, their genealogy, maritime activities, pearling traditions, or presence in Gulf emirates. The two entities - the medieval Egyptian Bahri Mamluks and the Arabian Gulf Al-Bahri tribe - appear to be completely distinct groups that happen to share a similar name derived from the Arabic word 'bahr' (sea/river).
data.nameEtymology: The name 'Bahri' derives from the Arabic word 'bahr' meaning sea or river, though the research only confirms this etymology for the Egyptian Bahri Mamluks, not the Arabian tribal group

data.history:
Al al-Ghawth represents one of the two main branches of the ancient Tayy tribe, descended from al-Ghawth, son of Julhumah ibn Udad (known as 'Tayy'). The Tayy genealogy traces through Julhumah's ancestry to Kahlan ibn Saba ibn Ya'rub, great-grandson of Qahtan, establishing clear Qahtani lineage. Originally based in Yemen, the Al al-Ghawth migrated with the broader Tayy confederation to northern Arabia in the late 2nd century CE, settling primarily in the mountain ranges of Aja and Salma (collectively known as Jabal Tayy, later Jabal Shammar). The immediate offspring of al-Ghawth's son 'Amr included Thu'al, Aswadan (known as Nabhan), Hani, Bawlan and Salaman, with the Banu Thu'al and Banu Nabhan becoming leading sub-branches in northern Arabia.

The late 6th century brought the devastating 25-year Fasad War between Al al-Ghawth and Al Jadila, resulting in significant tribal displacement. While Al Jadila migrated to Syria and converted to Christianity, Al al-Ghawth remained in Jabal Tayy, maintaining traditional beliefs until Islam's emergence. The tribe produced notable figures including the legendary poet and chieftain Hatim al-Ta'i, whose reputation for generosity became emblematic throughout Arabia. According to Islamic sources, Hatim al-Ta'i was mentioned by the famous mystic Ibn Arabi, who traced his own noble lineage from the tribes of Arabia, specifically noting that 'His relative Ḥātim aṭ-Ṭāʼiyy was well-known as a poet of pre-Islamic Arabia of the Qahtanite (South Arabian) tribe of Ṭayyi'.

During Islam's rise, prominent Al al-Ghawth leaders including Adi ibn Hatim (from Banu Thu'ayl) and Zayd al-Khayr (from Banu Nabhan) converted in 629-630, becoming companions of Prophet Muhammad. The tribe participated actively in the Ridda Wars, with Adi ibn Hatim playing a crucial diplomatic role in preventing tribal rebellion and securing Al al-Ghawth's allegiance to the nascent Islamic state. Throughout the Umayyad and Abbasid periods, Al al-Ghawth maintained their traditional territories while various sub-branches dispersed across the Arabian Peninsula and beyond, with descendants forming major tribal confederations including elements of the later Shammar and continuing to exist as active genealogical units in modern Arabian society.

Al-Jawasim is a branch of the larger Al-Dhafeer tribal confederation, classified under the Ahl al-Sumada division alongside six other branches: Al-Thira'an, Al-Ma'alem, Al-Ma'adin, Al-Oraif, Al-'Aljanat, and Al-Askar. According to multiple genealogical sources including Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi, Hamad al-Jassir, and Ibn Fadlallah al-Umari, Al-Dhafeer descends from Banu Lam, which itself traces back to the Tayy tribal lineage, making Al-Jawasim part of the broader Adnani Arab genealogical tree.

The ancestral origins of Al-Jawasim, through their Al-Dhafeer confederation, can be traced to areas near Medina, specifically a place called al-Dhaghn opposite Medina in the Hejaz region. The broader Al-Dhafeer tribe was known for their nomadic lifestyle, spreading throughout Arabia through raiding and migration rather than maintaining fixed settlements. They earned the distinctive nickname 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans' due to their practice of conducting raids accompanied by women and children, as well as their enthusiasm for military victories.

Historically, Al-Jawasim participated in significant regional conflicts through their Al-Dhafeer confederation. In 1782, when Bahrain was under Zand Persian control, the Al-Dhafeer tribe, including Al-Jawasim, sent cavalry and fighters to support Sheikh Ahmed bin Muhammad bin Khalifa and the House of Khalifa in confronting Persian rule. This culminated in the Battle of Zubarah, which resulted in victory for the Khalifa forces and their tribal allies, ending Persian control over Bahrain.

The tribe also played a role in defending against the Ikhwan movement in the early 20th century. On May 31, 1924, Al-Dhafeer forces, including Al-Jawasim, successfully defeated attacking Ikhwan forces from the Mutair tribe at the Battle of Qaysar in the southern desert of Iraq, killing 72 Ikhwan fighters including the notable leader Haif bin Shuqair al-Duwaish. Around 1926, following Ibn Saud's conquest of much of Arabia, the Al-Dhafeer confederation's Saudi Arabian branches were allowed to settle in various settlements after roads were secured, marking a transition from their traditional nomadic lifestyle.

data.history:
Al-Muntafiq was a large Arab tribal confederation that emerged in southern Iraq and Kuwait, with most tribes tracing their genealogy to Banu Uqayl of the ancient Banu Amir confederation from Najd. The confederation migrated to Iraq during the Islamic conquests and established control over the Basrah region under Ottoman suzerainty. The confederation is organized into three main branches: Bani Malik, al-Ajwad, and Bani Sa'id, with numerous sub-tribes under each division including notable groups like Albu Salih, Al Ibrahim, Al Usoom, and Al Shuwailat among many others.

Despite most members tracing ancestry to Banu Uqayl, the confederation's traditional leaders are Al-Saadun (the house of Saadun), who claim Sharifian descent from Mecca and remain Sunni Muslims. The al-Ajwad branch is said to partially originate from the ancient Arab tribe of Tayy. This diverse genealogical composition reflects the confederation's complex tribal structure built through centuries of alliances and intermarriage.

In 1521, Al-Muntafiq successfully occupied al-Ahsa and al-Qatif in eastern Saudi Arabia on behalf of the Ottomans before being expelled by Banu Khalid. During the Ottoman era, most confederation members transitioned from nomadic pastoralism to sedentary agricultural life in southern and western Iraq. From the late eighteenth century onwards, the majority converted to Shia Islam while their nominal leaders, the Al Saadun, remained Sunni.

The confederation held significant territorial influence, with the city of Nasiriya named after one of their sheikhs and the surrounding area known as 'Al-Muntafiq Province' until 1976. Led by figures like Yusuf Beg of the Saadun clan, they maintained traditional rivalries with the Dhufir confederation and Ibn Saud, though occasional cooperation occurred. After decades of sedentarization, tribal bonds have weakened considerably and Al Saadun leadership has become largely nominal. Today, many stateless 'bedoons' in Kuwait belong to this confederation, and the religious composition remains mixed between Sunni and Shia tribes, making it difficult to delineate precise membership boundaries.

data.history:
The Al Qasimi of Sharjah are the ruling dynasty of the Emirate of Sharjah in the United Arab Emirates, tracing their lineage through the Adnanite Arab genealogical tradition. According to Islamic and Arab genealogical sources, the Adnanites descend from Adnan, who is traditionally regarded as a descendant of Ishmael (Ismāʿīl), son of Abraham (Ibrahim). This places the Al Qasimi within the broader framework of Northern Arabian tribes, distinct from the Qahtanite Arabs of Southern Arabia who trace their lineage to Qahtan.

The Adnanite lineage holds particular significance in Islamic tradition, as the Prophet Muhammad also descended from this line through the Quraysh tribe. The genealogical chain from Adnan to Muhammad passes through Ma'add, Nizar, Mudar, and other ancestral figures who were considered chiefs ruling parts of the Arabian Peninsula. According to tradition, Adnan married Mahdad bint Laham and had two sons: Ma'add ibn Adnan, who became the ancestor of many Northern Arabian tribes, and Akk ibn Adnan, who migrated to Yemen and integrated with the Asharites.

The name Adnan appears in pre-Islamic Arabic poetry and North Arabian inscriptions, including Thamudic and Nabataean texts, where it held significance as an honorable ancestral figure. Pre-Islamic Arab poets such as Lubayb Ibn Rabi'a and Abbas Ibn Mirdas mentioned Adnan in their verses, and tribal groups used Adnanite ancestry as a source of pride and honor when competing with Qahtanite tribes. Some Nabataeans even named their children Abd Adnon (servant of Adnan), indicating the veneration accorded to this ancestral figure.

Historically, the distinction between Adnanites and Qahtanites formed a fundamental division in Arabian tribal identity, with the Adnanites representing the Northern Arabs of the Hejaz and other regions, while the Qahtanites were considered the original Arabs of Southern Arabia. However, some modern historians question whether this traditional distinction reflects historical reality or developed later during factional conflicts in the Umayyad period. The Al Qasimi's claim to Adnanite ancestry connects them to this broader network of Northern Arabian tribes that played crucial roles in pre-Islamic and Islamic Arabian history.

data.history:
Al Suhoob is one of the main branches of the Bani Khalid tribal confederation, which dominated eastern Arabia from the 15th to 18th centuries. The Bani Khalid confederation includes eleven main branches: Al Asfour, Al Humaid, Juboor, Du'um, Al Janah, Al Suhoob, Grusha, Al Musallam, 'Amayer, Al Subaih, and Mahashir & Nahood, with chieftainship traditionally held by the Al Humaid clan. The genealogical origins of Bani Khalid, including Al Suhoob, remain disputed among Arab genealogists, with claims ranging from descent from Khalid ibn al-Walid of Banu Makhzum (Quraysh), to origins in Banu Rabi'ah bin 'Amir bin Sa'sa'a from the Hawazin tribe, or from Banu Ghazia from the Tayy tribe.

As part of the Bani Khalid confederation, Al Suhoob participated in the tribal rule over al-Hasa and al-Qatif regions during the 15th-18th centuries. Under Barrak ibn Ghurayr of Al Humaid in 1670, the Bani Khalid expelled Ottoman forces and established the First Khalidi Emirate with its capital in Al-Mubarraz. The confederation controlled territory extending from Iraq in the north to the borders of Oman in the south, wielding significant political influence over Najd in central Arabia. The Bani Khalid maintained both Shia and Sunni Muslim members within their confederation.

The Bani Khalid, including Al Suhoob, became staunch enemies of the emerging Saudi state and the Wahhabi movement in the 18th century. They had initially ordered the emir of al-Uyayna to expel Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, forcing him to relocate to Diriyyah where he allied with the Al Saud. The confederation attempted to invade Nejd and Diriyyah to halt Saudi expansion, but their efforts failed. The Saudis conquered al-Hasa in 1793, deposing the Al 'Uray'ir rulers and ending the first period of Khalidi rule.

After the Ottoman restoration in 1818 and the brief reinstatement of Al 'Uray'ir rulers, the Bani Khalid faced internal quarrels over leadership and encroachment from rival tribes including the Ajman, Dawasir, Subay', and Mutayr. Despite forging an alliance with the Anizzah tribe, they were eventually defeated by tribal alliances led by the Mutayr and Ajman in 1823, and by the Subay' and Al Saud in 1830, definitively ending Khalidi political power. Many Bani Khalid clans, including Al Suhoob members, had already settled in al-Hasa and Nejd, while others migrated to Iraq, Lebanon, Palestine, Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, and the UAE following their military defeats.

data.history: The Alhasasana are documented as a branch of the Mahamid tribe, which traces its origins to the Khawlaniyah al-Qahtaniyah Harb confederation. The Mahamid originally resided in Yemen and the Hijaz region between Mecca and Medina before spreading throughout the Arabian Peninsula and beyond. The tribal biography indicates that when the Mahamid population grew too large for their traditional territories in Yemen, Najd, and Hijaz, tribal councils convened to decide their course of action. Rather than engage in warfare with neighboring Harb clans, they chose migration as their solution. The Mahamid migration path took them first to Dumat Al-Jandal in northern Arabia, where they settled temporarily before moving toward the Levant. They eventually reached the Karak region where they established significant presence, forming alliances with local clans including Al-Omaro Bani Uqba. The Mahamid established the Emirate of Mahamid, primarily led by the Al-Amru clan, which ruled for an extended period until various clans united against their rule due to reported harsh governance. From their Levantine settlements, the Mahamid dispersed across multiple regions, with the Alhasasana branch specifically documented as emerging in Palestine alongside other sub-tribes including Kiawan, Alkhudur, Alsawalima, Alhamamada, Almusaeada, Aljeayisa, Dar Alearabi, Al'asead, and Al'ahmad. The broader Mahamid network extended across the Arabian Peninsula, Levant, Iraq, and Arab Africa, maintaining their tribal identity across these diverse geographical regions.

data.history:
The `Amarat represents a northern branch of the great Anizah tribe, one of the most prominent tribal confederations in Arabian Peninsula history. The Anizah, to which `Amarat belongs, traces its genealogical origins to the Rabi'a branch of the Adnanites, alongside tribes like Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib. Classical Arab genealogists placed Anizah within this large Rabiʿa branch, with Anizah's eponymous ancestor being a great uncle to the ancestors of these related tribes. Historical records show that Anizah existed as an autonomous tribal group predating the rise of Islam in the seventh century, with two main historical branches - one nomadic in the northern Arabian steppes bordering Syria and Mesopotamia, and another sedentary in the wadis of Al-Yamama in eastern Najd.

The modern `Amarat branch became prominent during the Ottoman era as part of the broader Anizah expansion northward into Mesopotamia and the Levant. According to British intelligence sources from the early 20th century, the `Amarat were centered in the deserts of Iraq, where they operated as semi-nomadic Bedouins. During World War I, British political officer Gerard Leachman had close relationships with Fahad Bey Ibn Haddal, chief of the Amarat Bedouins, and documented their alliance with the Muntafiq Bedouin federation during this critical period.

The establishment of modern Middle Eastern borders in the early 20th century severely impacted the traditional Bedouin lifestyle patterns of the `Amarat, which had historically involved movement across vast areas spanning multiple modern states. Like other Anizah branches, the `Amarat were forced to adapt to these new political realities, with many eventually settling within the newly established nation-states and taking on formal citizenship. Their presence in Iraq during the early 1900s represents part of the larger historical pattern of Anizah tribal movement from their original territories in the Arabian Peninsula into the fertile regions of Mesopotamia, a migration that had been ongoing since medieval times but reached its peak during the Ottoman period.

The research provided does not contain sufficient information about Bani Ghafir to construct a comprehensive historical narrative. The only research text available discusses Sanhan and Bani Bahlul District in Yemen's Sana'a Governorate, which is unrelated to Bani Ghafir. While the existing data references Bani Ghafir as part of the Ghafiri confederation in Oman and mentions historical conflicts with the Hinawi confederation under Mohammed bin Nasir Alghafiri's leadership, none of this information appears in the provided research text and therefore cannot be verified or expanded upon.

Bani Malik represents multiple tribal confederations across the Arabian Peninsula, most prominently tracing descent from Malik al-Ashtar al-Nakh'ei, a renowned military commander who fought alongside Ali ibn Abi Talib during the early Islamic period. The historical Malik al-Ashtar belonged to the Nakh'a tribe, itself an offshoot of the larger Madhhij confederation from South Arabia. According to genealogical sources, these groups claim Qahtani origins through a complex lineage connecting them to the ancient Azd tribal confederation, specifically through the Khuza'a branch that settled in Makkah and fought alongside the Prophet Muhammad against the Banu Bakr.

The Al-Ali branch emerged as one of the most significant offshoots of Bani Malik, establishing themselves across Iraq, the Arabian Gulf, and beyond by the 16th century. In Iraq, their territories extended from al-Diwaniyah in the north to al-Rumaitha in the south, with settlements concentrated along the Euphrates River system including the al-Rafi, al-Yusufyia, and al-Lawah rivers. When these waterways dried up around 1900, many Al-Ali families relocated to areas around the al-Hindyiah branch of the Euphrates River. The tribe was organized into multiple sub-branches including Al-Faraj (the largest), Al-Ismail, Al-Awabid, Al-Humaidat, Al-Ibrahim, and Bani Rzaij.

The tribe's political influence reached its zenith through figures like Muhammad Hasan Abi al-Mahasin, a prominent poet and politician who led revolutionary activities against British occupation during and after World War I. He served as president of the Revolutionary Council in 1920 and later as Minister of Education under King Faisal I in 1923. His grandson, Nouri al-Maliki, would later serve as Iraq's Prime Minister from 2006-2014. The Kashif Al-Ghita'a clerical dynasty also emerged from Al-Ali lineages, with Shaikh Ja'afar earning the title 'Kashif Al-Ghita'a' meaning 'revealer of religion and knowledge.'

Historically, Bani Malik lived in various regions including Ahwaz, Qatif, Bahrain, and Iraq, with different branches establishing themselves in Basrah, Nasiriyah, and along the eastern bank of the Euphrates River. By the late 16th century, most Al-Ali tribe members had migrated from what is now central Saudi Arabia to neighboring countries including UAE, Qatar, Kuwait, Bahrain, Iraq, and Jordan. Contemporary Bani Malik groups remain active across their traditional territories in Saudi Arabia, Iraq, Bahrain, Qatar, Kuwait, and the UAE, maintaining both pastoral and settled lifestyles while preserving their genealogical connections to the original Malik al-Ashtar lineage.

Banu Al Du'al represents a complex tribal entity with dual genealogical affiliations in Arabian Peninsula history. According to the research, they are identified as one of the four clans of Bakr ibn 'Abd Manat (alongside Banu Damrah, Banu Laith, and Banu Mudlij), which places them within the Adnanite lineage through Bakr ibn 'Abd Manat ibn Kinana ibn Khuzaymah ibn Mudrikah ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan. However, they are also described as a tribal branch within the Zahrani confederation, which traces to the Qahtani lineage through Zahran ibn Ka'ab ibn Al-Harith ibn Ka'ab ibn Abdullah Ibn Mālik ibn Nasr ibn Al-Azd. This dual affiliation reflects the complex nature of Arabian tribal genealogies and suggests either multiple tribal branches bearing the same name or intricate historical alliances and integrations.

Historically, Banu Al Du'al established a significant presence in Mecca where they formed crucial strategic alliances. Their most notable partnership was with Al-Jadara, a Zahranid family known as 'the Wall-Masons' whose progenitor, Amer ibn Amr ibn Ja'thama, was reportedly the first to reconstruct the walls of the Kaaba after Abraham and Ishmael. This alliance extended to include the Quraysh tribe, creating a powerful coalition in pre-Islamic Mecca. The pinnacle of these inter-tribal relationships was achieved through a pivotal marital alliance when Kilab ibn Murrah of Quraysh married Fatimah bint Sa'd Al-Jadari, producing two sons: Zuhrah and Qusay ibn Kilab.

The significance of this marital alliance cannot be overstated in Arabian history. Qusay ibn Kilab became a direct ancestor of Prophet Muhammad and established lineages that would later produce the third and fourth Rashidun caliphs (Uthman and Ali), as well as the founding dynasties of the Umayyad, Abbasid, and Fatimid caliphates. This connection places Banu Al Du'al, through their alliance network, at the heart of Islamic political and religious history. As part of the Bakr ibn 'Abd Manat confederation, they were maternal cousins to the Quraysh, though this relationship included periods of both cooperation and rivalry, including conflicts such as the battle of Dhat Naqif.

Religiously, Banu Al Du'al originally practiced South Arabian polytheism like other Bakr ibn 'Abd Manat clans. They converted to Islam following the Conquest of Mecca in 630 CE, when the majority of the Bakr ibn 'Abd Manat tribal confederation embraced the new faith. Their current status appears to be largely historical, with descendants likely integrated into modern Zahrani communities and other related tribal groups throughout the Arabian Peninsula, particularly in the Hijaz region, though some descendants may also be found in Jordan and Egypt as indicated for other Bakr ibn 'Abd Manat clans.

Banu Hanzala was a major branch of the Tamim tribal confederation, descended from Hanzala ibn Malik ibn Zayd Manat ibn Tamim ibn Murr ibn Udd ibn Amr ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar ibn Nizar ibn Ma'ad ibn Adnan. As an Adnanite tribe, they traced their ultimate ancestry through Adnan to Ishmael, son of Abraham, placing them among the northern Arabian tribal groups distinct from the Qahtanite Arabs of southern Arabia. According to genealogical tradition, Hanzala had eight sons: Malik, Yarbu', Rabi'a, 'Amr, Murra, Ghalib, Kalfa and Qays, from whom descended the important sub-clans of Banu Darim, Banu Yarbu', Banu Tahiuh, and Barajim.

Historically, Banu Hanzala inhabited al-Yamama in central Arabia and maintained a strong presence throughout Najd and the eastern Arabian Peninsula. During the pre-Islamic period, they actively participated in the Ayyam al-Arab (Days of the Arabs) tribal conflicts, most notably achieving a significant victory at the battle of Yawm Dhi Najab against Banu Amir and the Kindite king Akil al-Murar, who was killed in the engagement while many Banu Amir leaders were captured. As part of the greater Banu Tamim confederation, they came into contact with the Prophet Muhammad in the 8th year of Hijrah but did not immediately convert to Islam, reflecting the cautious approach many tribal groups took toward the new religious movement.

Following the Islamic conquests, Banu Hanzala maintained their position within the Tamim tribal structure as the broader confederation spread across the Arabian Peninsula and into newly conquered territories. Historical records indicate that during the Sasanian period, several clans of Hanzala were transplanted by Shapur II to Tavvaz, near present-day Dālakī in Fars province of Iran, demonstrating their early involvement in trans-regional migrations. The tribe's influence was subsequently embedded within the larger Tamim political and tribal networks rather than operating as an independent political entity, with their descendants spreading throughout the Islamic world as part of the Tamim diaspora.

Notable descendants of Banu Hanzala include prominent religious scholars and ruling families that emerged from the broader Tamim confederation. These include the House of Al Thani, the ruling family of Qatar since 1847, the Al ash-Sheikh family who have served as Grand Muftis of Saudi Arabia, and influential religious figures such as Ishaq ibn Rahwayh and Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab, the founder of the Nejdi Salafi movement. The tribe's genealogical significance is also reflected in the presence of Tamim tribal elements, including Hanzala branches, among Iranian Arabs in Khuzestan and Fars provinces, where they settled alongside other Arab tribes such as Banu Ka'b and Banu Hammed, maintaining their Arab identity and tribal affiliations over centuries.

data.history: Based on the research provided, there appears to be significant confusion in the existing data between two distinct entities: the historical Banu Makhzum clan of Quraysh from Mecca, and the Arabian Peninsula tribe known as Banu Khalid that established an emirate in eastern Arabia. The research focuses entirely on Banu Makhzum and Khalid ibn al-Walid, not the Banu Khalid tribe mentioned in the existing data. Banu Makhzum was one of the three most powerful clans in pre-Islamic Mecca alongside Banu Hashim and Banu Umayya, dominated by the al-Mughira family line and known for their wealth from trade routes to Yemen and Ethiopia. They initially opposed Islam under Abu Jahl's leadership but eventually converted, with Khalid ibn al-Walid becoming a prominent Muslim military commander around 627-629 CE. The Banu Makhzum's male line reportedly ended around 750 CE during the Umayyad collapse when Khalid's descendants died in a plague in Syria. The research mentions that the Arabian Peninsula's Banu Khalid tribe established a 17th-19th century emirate, but provides no verified information about their origins, genealogy, or historical development beyond what was already in the existing data. This represents a clear disconnect between the research provided (focusing on the Meccan Qurayshi clan) and the subject tribe (the eastern Arabian Banu Khalid).

The Banu Mustaliq were a pre-Islamic Arabian tribe belonging to the larger Banu Khuza'a confederation, with their lineage tracing back to the Azdi Qahtanite tribal structure. According to genealogical sources, they were descended from Khuza'a through Amr ibn Luhay, though there is scholarly debate about whether Khuza'a itself originated from Qahtanite or Adnanite lineage. The tribe occupied the strategic territory of Qadid (also called Qudayd) on the Red Sea shore between Jeddah and Rabigh in the Hijaz region, where they controlled vital water sources and maintained a pastoral lifestyle with substantial herds of camels, sheep, and goats.

As a branch of the Banu Khuza'a confederation, the Banu Mustaliq initially maintained alliances with the Quraysh of Mecca before Islam. Their historical significance centers on their encounter with the early Muslim community in December 627 CE (8 AH) during the expedition of al-Muraysi. Following reports that the Banu Mustaliq were preparing attacks against Muhammad, a Muslim force met them in battle at a watering place called al-Muraysi. The battle resulted in a decisive Muslim victory, with the capture of the tribal chief al-Harith and 200 families, along with substantial booty including 200 camels and 5,000 sheep and goats. The Mustaliq lost ten men while only one Muslim was killed.

Among the captives was al-Harith's daughter Juwayriya bint al-Harith, whose husband Mustafa bin Safwan had been killed in the battle. She initially fell to Muhammad's companion Thabit ibn Qays ibn Al-Shammas but was later freed when Muhammad proposed marriage to her in 627-628 CE. This strategic marriage led to the release of nearly 100 Mustaliq families, as the Muslims considered it inappropriate to hold as slaves the kinsmen of the Prophet's wife. The tribal integration into the early Islamic state marked the end of their independent political existence, though they maintained their identity as a branch of the Banu Khuza'a confederation.

The defeat of the Banu Mustaliq represented part of Muhammad's broader military strategy during the Muslim-Quraysh War, where he shifted focus from raiding Quraysh caravans to subjugating northern Arab tribes. The tribe's incorporation into the Islamic state through both military conquest and marriage alliance exemplified the dual approach of force and diplomacy used in early Islamic expansion. While the Banu Mustaliq ceased to exist as an independent political entity after 627 CE, their integration contributed to the broader absorption of Arabian tribal confederations into the emerging Islamic polity.

Banu Sulaym was a major Arabian tribe of Adnanite origin, descended from Sulaym ibn Mansur ibn Ikrima ibn Khasafa ibn Qays Aylan. The tribe was divided into three main divisions: Imru' al-Qays (the strongest division with branches Khufaf, Awf, and Bahz), Harith (with branches Mu'awiyah, Zafar, Rifa'a, Ka'b, and Abs), and Tha'laba (including Malik and Dhakwan branches). In pre-Islamic times, they inhabited the northern Hejaz with the Harrah volcanic field (formerly called Harrat Bani Sulaym) forming their territorial heart. The Imru' al-Qays division controlled the eastern slopes where the Bahz branch owned lucrative gold mines, while the Harith occupied the western slopes. Some Mu'awiyah branch members lived in Medina and converted to Judaism before the arrival of the Aws and Khazraj.

The Dhakwan clan maintained close ties with the Quraysh of Mecca, with members serving important roles including al-Hakim ibn Umayya as muhtasib of pre-Islamic Mecca. Initially hostile to Muhammad and fighting alongside the Quraysh at the Battle of the Trench in 627, most Sulaym converted to Islam by 630 and fought with Muhammad at the Battle of Hunayn. However, most defected during the Ridda Wars after Muhammad's death in 632. Following the Muslim conquests, Sulaym tribesmen migrated to northern Syria, the Jazira (Upper Mesopotamia), and various cities including Kufa, Basra, and throughout Khurasan, though maintaining presence in their Arabian homeland.

As members of the Qays tribal confederation, they opposed the Umayyads and suffered heavy losses at the Battle of Marj Rahit in 684, with about 600 Sulaym killed. Under Dhakwan chief Umayr ibn al-Hubab, they later contributed to Umayyad defeats and engaged in territorial conflicts with the Taghlib tribe along the Khabur River until 693. In the 9th century, much of the Arabian Sulaym were absorbed by the Harb tribe from Yemen. The Fatimid caliph al-Aziz expelled the Sulaym and Banu Hilal to Upper Egypt around 975-996 for supporting the Qarmatians.

In the mid-11th century, prolonged famine in Egypt prompted their westward migration with the Hilal into Libya, where Sulaym established themselves primarily in Cyrenaica. Their descendants, known as Sa'ada, continue to dominate eastern Libya today, divided into Harabi (including Ubaydat, Bara'asa, Hasa, Derasa, Aylat Fayid) and Jabarina (including Awaqir, Magharba, Majabira, Aryibat, Baraghith) divisions. The Al Bin Ali clan of the Bani Utbah confederation traces its lineage to Banu Sulaym, distinguishing them from other Utub clans who descend from Anizah. This branch participated in the 1783 conquest of Bahrain and various military campaigns including the 1837 conquest of Mombasa.

data.history: The Bariq tribe traces its lineage to Bariq ibn Uday ibn Haritha ibn Amr Muzayqiya ibn Amir Ma' al-Sama ibn Haritha al-Ghatraf ibn Imru' al-Qays ibn Tha'laba ibn Mazin ibn al-Azd, establishing them as a prominent Qahtani Azdi tribe. Originally inhabiting Ma'rib, the capital of the Sabaean Kingdom in Yemen, their lands were irrigated by the famous Ma'rib Dam. When the dam collapsed for the third time in the 3rd century AD, large numbers of the Bariq tribe migrated northward and settled on a mountain called Bariq in the Tihama region of southwestern Saudi Arabia, from which they derived their name. The tribe established itself in mountainous regions between Hijaz and Asir, creating settlements in areas like Wadi Bariq and surrounding highlands. Bariq consists of two main divisions: Al-Humaydah and Al-Ali, with Al-Humaydah further subdivided into six groups (Al-Hajri, Al-Salim, Maha'mula, Al-Aram, Al-Gdraymah, and Al-Fseel), while Al-Ali comprises three groups (Al-Musa ibn Ali, Al-Isba'i, and Al-Jabali). During the early Islamic period, Bariq was among the first Arab tribes to embrace Islam and received direct correspondence from Prophet Muhammad, who granted them special privileges over their lands and resources in a letter that guaranteed their territorial rights and established rules for hospitality toward Muslim travelers. The tribe actively participated in the Muslim conquests and many Bariqis settled in various countries after the expansion. The tribe's territory in modern Saudi Arabia extends from 15 miles north of Mahayil, stretching 20 miles north-south and 30 miles east-west, bounded by Banu Shihr to the east, Khath'm and Balqarn to the north, Al-Raysh and Al-Durayb to the south, and Rabi'at al-Maqatirah to the west. The Humaydah clan, numbering about 7,000 men including 4,000 nomads, became the dominant political force in the region and governed the city of Bareq. During the rise of the First Saudi State, the Bariq tribe gave allegiance in 1809 under the leadership of chief Ahmed Ibn Zahir of the Humaydah clan. They continued resistance against Egyptian forces after 1818 and later adapted to Ottoman rule from 1872. In the early 20th century, the region came under Idrisi rule before ultimately being incorporated into the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia in 1924.

This large movement out of Yemen has been dated to the late 3rd century CE, leading them to settle on a mountain called Bariq in Tihama from which they derived their name.

data.history:
The Dhahuriyiin are an Arab tribal group historically associated with the Hajar Mountain region of the northern UAE, particularly around Ras Al Khaimah. They are closely linked with the Shihuh and Habus tribes, sharing cultural similarities and traditions with these neighboring mountain tribes. The research indicates they were part of the broader tribal confederation that inhabited the rugged Hajar Mountains, where they likely maintained traditional mountain lifestyles similar to their associated tribes.

The Dhahuriyiin appear to have been involved in the complex territorial arrangements of the Hajar Mountain region, where tribal boundaries were traditionally defined by wadis and mountain ranges. Their connection to the Habus tribe suggests they may have shared similar cultural practices, though the extent of this relationship and their specific territorial holdings are not clearly documented in available sources.

Like other Hajar Mountain tribes, the Dhahuriyiin may have spoken the distinctive Shehhi dialect of Arabic, which is thought to have Himyarite Yemeni origins dating back to the second century BCE, though this cannot be definitively confirmed from the research. Their current status and specific geographic distribution within the broader UAE region remains unclear from available historical records.

The limited documentation of the Dhahuriyiin reflects the broader challenge of reconstructing the histories of smaller tribal groups in the Arabian Peninsula, where oral traditions and genealogical records may not have been as extensively preserved or documented as those of larger, more politically prominent tribes.

Al-Farei'ab (الفريعاب) is a clan within the Manasir al-Nil (Riverain Manasir), one of the Sunni Arab riverine tribes of Northern Sudan. They inhabit the region of the Fourth Cataract of the Nile in their homeland called Dar al-Manasir, alongside other riverine clans including al-Wahabab, al-Suleimaniyah, al-Kabanah, al-Diqeisab, al-Hamsab, al-Ga'al, al-'Ababsah, al-Hamdatiab, and al-'Amasib. The Manasir people are distinct from the Arabian Peninsula Manasir based in the United Arab Emirates, representing instead an indigenous Nile culture that adopted Islam and Arabic as their first language.

According to current Manasir oral tradition, the tribe traces its origins to an ancestor named Mansur, though multiple genealogical theories exist. Some Manasir in Gezira claim descent from Mansur bin Qahtan belonging to the Southern Arabs (Qahtaniin), while some historians trace their origin to Mansur bin 'Aim of the Kawahla. Another theory suggests they descend from cousins of the Shaiqi clan al-Hankab who migrated after internal conflicts, with Mansur being a direct descendant of King Sabir. Critical research indicates that the current Manasir community, including al-Farei'ab, represents a voluntary amalgamation over centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs, and recruited tribal minorities.

As a riverain clan, al-Farei'ab pursues small-scale agriculture on alluvial soils along the Nile, with date cultivation as their primary cash crop sold on national markets. They share the material culture common to Manasir people, including skills in building mud houses (Galus) and historically floating wood from the Atbarah region. Like other Manasir, they do not have unique tribal marks (Shilukh) but copy the scarification patterns of either upstream or downstream neighboring tribes. The clan was among those affected by the construction of the Merowe Dam, which impacted all villages in Dar al-Manasir, with the Manasir population in the region totaling approximately 33,000 residents and 17,000 non-residents as of 1992-1993.

folkLegends.story: The Manasir living in Gezira claim that their apical forefather is called Mansur bin Qahtan, belonging to the Southern Arabs called Qahtaniin.

folkLegends.story: Some historians trace the origin of the Manasir back to the Kawahla, saying that they are sons of Mansur bin 'Aim.

folkLegends.story: They are descendants of a group of cousins from a Shaiqi clan called al-Hankab, who had to migrate after internal fights, with Mansur being a direct descendant of King Sabir.

data.history:
The Habus is an Arab tribe of Qahtani lineage primarily settled in Ras Al Khaimah, UAE, around Khatt, Fahlain and the Hajar Mountain wadis. Their name derives from their original holdings on Jebel Hibs mountain in Ras Al Khaimah. The tribe speaks the distinctive Shehhi dialect of Arabic, which has Himyarite Yemeni origins dating to the second century BCE, shared with neighboring Shihuh and Dhahuriyiin tribes.

As an agrarian mountain people, the Habus cultivated extensive farmland in the wadis of the Hajar Mountains, particularly Wadi Naqab and Wadi Bih. The area around Wadi Naqab was originally held by the Naqbiyin tribe, but was gradually acquired by the Habus after the Naqbiyin fell out of favor with local rulers in the pre-Qawasim period (1695-1740), with many Naqbiyin relocating to Dibba and Khor Fakkan. By 1800, Wadi Naqab was considered Habus territory.

Fiercely independent, the Habus successfully resisted taxation attempts by Sheikh Sultan bin Salim Al Qasimi, ruler of Ras Al Khaimah (1921-1948), leading to a brief conflict and subsequent agreement between Habus Sheikh Hamdan bin Malik and Sultan bin Salim. A copy of their peace agreement was lodged with Sultan Qaboos of Oman upon Hamdan's death. In 1951, the Habus became involved in territorial disputes when the Wali of Bukha claimed their areas as Omani territory. They initially accepted this but later agreed to Ras Al Khaimah rule under Sheikh Saqr bin Mohammed Al Qasimi in 1955, though continued resistance into the 1960s included holding up mineral survey parties and attempting to assert independence in 1963.

The tribe traded wheat, honey, goats, dairy products and firewood in Ras Al Khaimah town, purchasing metal tools, coffee, salt and clothing. They expanded their date plantations around Khatt in the 1950s-1960s through purchases and rentals. Though many mountain settlements are now abandoned, younger tribe members have returned to build summer houses, and a tribal festival established in 2010 showcases their traditions, handicrafts and perfumery.

data.history:
The Hashid is one of Yemen's most ancient and influential tribal confederations, with origins tracing back over two millennia to the Sabaean period. First mentioned in Sabaean inscriptions as 'Hashdum' in the 4th century BCE, the tribe was part of the Sum'ay confederation alongside Humlan and Yarsum tribes, worshipping the god Ta'lab. According to medieval Yemeni genealogies, Hashid and their traditional rivals Bakil were sons of Jashim bin Jubran bin Nawf, descended from the ancient Hamdan tribe. The Banu Hamdan served as qayls (chiefs) of Hashid in Sabaean times and later acquired control over parts of Bakil, giving their name to the broader Hamdan confederation that included both Hashid and Bakil tribes. In the late 3rd century, the Hamdan confederation switched allegiance to the Himyarite kingdom.

The tribe's conversion to Islam came in 622 CE when Muhammad sent Ali ibn Abi Talib to Yemen after Khalid ibn al-Walid's unsuccessful mission. Unlike many Arabian tribes, the Hamdan remained loyal to Islam after Muhammad's death and did not join the ridda (apostasy) movement. They maintained allegiance to Ali ibn Abi Talib throughout his conflicts with the Umayyads, and by the 10th century had invited Imam al-Hadi Yahya bin al-Hussain to establish Zaidi rule in northern Yemen at Sa'da in 893-897 CE. This established the Hashid and Bakil as the 'two wings' of the Zaidi imamate, a relationship that would define their political role for centuries. Throughout Islamic history, Hashid warriors participated in major conquests and battles, with prominent figures like Saeed bin Qais Al-Hamdani carrying the Hamdan banner in the battles of the Camel and Siffin.

The confederation played crucial roles in Yemen's medieval politics, supporting various Zaidi imams while maintaining significant autonomy in their northern highland territories around Sanaa. In modern times, the Hashid confederation was dominated for decades by the powerful al-Ahmar family, particularly Sheikh Abdullah ibn Husayn al-Ahmar who formed a crucial alliance with President Ali Abdullah Saleh from 1978 until his death in 2007. This alliance made al-Ahmar Yemen's second most powerful figure as Speaker of Parliament. However, following the Arab Spring in 2011, his sons led the Hashid in an insurgency against Saleh, playing an essential role in the revolution that forced the president to step down in 2012. The subsequent conflict with the Houthis and loss of Saudi support led to internal divisions within the confederation, with many Hashid tribes reaching separate peace agreements or switching sides, marking a significant decline in their unified political influence.
